name: Confluent Certified Developer for Apache Kafka

questions.q: A topic has 6 partitions. Which ordering guarantee does Kafka provide for its records?
options: Total order across the whole topic
Order is guaranteed only within each partition
questions.explanation: Each partition is an ordered append-only log, but there is no coordination of order ACROSS partitions. Records that must be ordered relative to each other must share a partition — which is why they are given the same key.

questions.q: All events for a given customer must be processed in order. How is this achieved when producing?
options: Use the customer ID as the message key so all of a customer's events hash to the same partition
Use a single-partition topic for all customers
questions.explanation: Keyed records are partitioned by murmur2(key) % numPartitions, so one customer's events always land on the same partition and stay ordered. A single partition would also work but destroys parallelism for all customers.

questions.q: A consumer group with 8 consumers reads a topic with 6 partitions. What happens?
options: Two consumers remain idle with no partitions assigned
All 8 consumers share partitions in round-robin per record
questions.explanation: Within a group each partition goes to exactly one consumer, so at most 6 consumers can be active on 6 partitions — the extra 2 get nothing. Partition count is the group's maximum parallelism.

questions.q: A topic is configured with replication.factor=3 and min.insync.replicas=2, and producers use acks=all. What does this setup guarantee?
options: Writes succeed even if all three replicas are down
Every acknowledged write exists on at least 2 replicas, and the topic tolerates one broker failure without losing acknowledged data
questions.explanation: acks=all waits for all in-sync replicas, and min.insync.replicas=2 refuses writes if fewer than 2 are in sync. One broker can fail (ISR=2 still meets the minimum); acknowledged data always exists on ≥2 replicas. This is the canonical durability configuration.

questions.q: With min.insync.replicas=2 on an RF=3 topic, two brokers holding replicas of a partition go down. What do producers using acks=all experience?
options: Writes continue normally against the single remaining replica
Writes fail with NotEnoughReplicas errors until the ISR recovers
Data is silently redirected to another topic
Consumers also stop being able to read
questions.explanation: The ISR (1) is below min.insync.replicas (2), so acks=all produces are rejected — availability is sacrificed to protect durability. Reads of already-committed data can continue from the remaining leader.

questions.q: Which statement describes log compaction (cleanup.policy=compact)?
options: Records older than retention.ms are deleted regardless of key
Kafka eventually retains at least the most recent value for each key; a null-value record (tombstone) marks a key for deletion
The log is compressed with zstd
questions.explanation: Compaction is per-key retention of the latest value — turning a topic into a durable changelog (great for table-like state). Tombstones delete keys. Time/size deletion is the 'delete' policy; consumption never removes data.

questions.q: Two different applications, each with its own group.id, consume the same topic. How is data delivered?
options: The applications split the records between them
Each application (group) receives ALL records; within each group, partitions are divided among its consumers
Only the first group to subscribe receives data
Both groups must have identical consumer counts
questions.explanation: Consumer groups are independent — Kafka is pub-sub across groups and queue-like within a group. Each group maintains its own offsets in __consumer_offsets.

questions.q: Which combination of client settings secures a connection to a SASL/SCRAM-secured cluster over TLS?
options: security.protocol=SASL_SSL, sasl.mechanism=SCRAM-SHA-512, sasl.jaas.config with the credentials, plus a truststore for the broker certificates
questions.explanation: SASL_SSL = SASL authentication over a TLS-encrypted channel; the mechanism and JAAS config carry the SCRAM credentials, and the truststore validates the brokers. ACLs then authorize the authenticated principal.

questions.q: A consumer application is denied with a TopicAuthorizationException although the topic exists. What is the most likely cause?
options: The topic has too many partitions
The client's principal lacks the required ACLs (e.g., Read on the topic and Read on the consumer group)
The consumer uses the wrong compression codec
questions.explanation: Authorization errors are ACL problems: a consuming principal needs Read on the topic AND Read on its group. Authentication succeeded (otherwise the error would be an authentication failure).

questions.q: What replaaced ZooKeeper's role in modern Kafka clusters (Kafka 4.x)?
options: KRaft — an internal Raft-based controller quorum managing cluster metadata
questions.explanation: KRaft mode has Kafka manage its own metadata via a Raft consensus quorum of controllers — no external ZooKeeper. Client APIs are unaffected; it's a cluster architecture change.

questions.q: Partitions are added to an existing topic whose producers use keyed messages. What is the consequence?
options: Nothing — Kafka rebalances old data automatically
New records for a key may map to a DIFFERENT partition than that key's older records, breaking per-key ordering across the boundary
Producers must restart before any records flow
questions.explanation: Partitioning is hash(key) % numPartitions — changing numPartitions changes the mapping. Existing data stays where it was, so a key's history spans partitions and ordered consumption per key is broken across the change. Plan partition counts up front for keyed topics.

questions.q: What are Kafka message headers for?
options: Determining which partition a record goes to
Carrying metadata (correlation IDs, trace context, schema hints) alongside the key and value without affecting partitioning
questions.explanation: Headers are key/value metadata attached to records — used for tracing, routing hints, and provenance. Partitioning uses the message key, not headers.

questions.q: What does enable.idempotence=true on a producer actually prevent?
options: Duplicates caused by the application calling send() twice
Duplicates caused by the producer's own retries after transient failures, while preserving per-partition ordering
Duplicates on the consumer side after rebalances
questions.explanation: Idempotence gives each producer session sequence numbers so broker-side retry duplicates are discarded. It does NOT dedupe application-level re-sends nor consumer-side reprocessing — those need transactions or idempotent consumers.

questions.q: A consume-transform-produce application must achieve exactly-once semantics from input topic to output topic. What is required?
options: Kafka transactions: a transactional.id producer sending output AND committing consumed offsets in the same transaction, with downstream consumers using isolation.level=read_committed
questions.explanation: EOS for read-process-write means the output records and the input offset commit are atomic — that's sendOffsetsToTransaction inside a transaction. read_committed consumers then ignore aborted attempts. Idempotence alone only covers producer retry duplicates.

questions.q: A consumer with enable.auto.commit=true crashes AFTER auto-commit ran but BEFORE it finished processing the last polled batch. What happens to those records?
options: They are lost to this group — the committed offset already moved past them
They are moved to a dead letter queue
questions.explanation: Auto-commit can commit offsets for records not yet processed, creating an at-most-once window. To get at-least-once, commit manually AFTER processing (commitSync/commitAsync).

questions.q: A consumer processing takes up to 10 minutes per batch, and the group keeps rebalancing with 'consumer left the group' messages. Which setting is being violated?
options: max.poll.interval.ms — too much time passes between poll() calls; raise it or reduce max.poll.records
questions.explanation: Heartbeats run on a background thread, so session.timeout isn't the issue — the poll-interval timer is. If poll() isn't called within max.poll.interval.ms (default 5 min), the consumer is evicted. Raise the limit, shrink batches, or speed up processing.

questions.q: When does auto.offset.reset=earliest take effect?
options: Only when the group has no valid committed offset for a partition (new group, expired offsets, or out-of-range)
questions.explanation: auto.offset.reset is the FALLBACK position. With valid committed offsets, consumption always resumes from them — earliest/latest only applies when no usable offset exists.

questions.q: Which producer configuration change increases throughput at the cost of a little latency?
options: Increase linger.ms (e.g., 5–100 ms) and batch.size so larger, better-compressed batches are sent
questions.explanation: linger.ms deliberately waits to fill batches; bigger batches amortize request overhead and compress better — the standard throughput knob. acks=0 trades DURABILITY (not just latency) and isn't a batching improvement.

questions.q: In Schema Registry, subject 'orders-value' uses BACKWARD compatibility. What does registering a new schema version require, and which side upgrades first?
options: New schema must be readable by the OLD schema; producers upgrade first
Consumers using the NEW schema must be able to read data written with the PREVIOUS schema (e.g., deleted fields / added optional fields); consumers upgrade first
questions.explanation: BACKWARD = new reader, old data. Consumers deploy the new schema first and can still read what current producers write; then producers may upgrade. FORWARD is the reverse (producers first); FULL is both.

questions.q: How does a consumer know which Avro schema a record was written with, using the Confluent wire format?
options: The full schema is embedded in every message
Each message carries a schema ID; the deserializer fetches (and caches) that schema from Schema Registry
The topic name encodes the schema
Schemas are guessed from the bytes
questions.explanation: The wire format is magic byte + 4-byte schema ID + payload — compact messages, with the schema itself stored once in the registry and cached client-side after first fetch.

questions.q: A producer intermittently logs NotLeaderForPartitionException but messages eventually arrive. What is happening?
options: The topic is corrupt and must be recreated
A retriable error: partition leadership moved (broker restart/rebalance); the producer refreshes metadata and retries automatically
questions.explanation: Leadership changes are normal transient cluster events — classic RETRIABLE errors the client handles with retries + metadata refresh. Serialization and record-too-large errors are non-retriable and would fail every attempt.

questions.q: A consumer hits a record that always throws SerializationException, blocking the partition. What is the standard remedy?
options: Restart the consumer until it works
Catch/handle the poison record: skip past its offset (or use an error-tolerant deserializer) and publish it to a dead-letter topic for analysis
questions.explanation: Deserialization failures are non-retriable — the same bytes fail forever, so retrying blocks the partition. Skip-and-DLQ keeps the pipeline flowing while preserving the bad record for investigation.

questions.q: Which tool shows a consumer group's current offset, log-end offset, and lag per partition from the command line?
questions.explanation: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe lists each partition's CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET and LAG (plus member assignments) — also the tool for --reset-offsets. kafka-topics.sh describes topic structure, not group positions.

questions.q: Where is producer-side compression applied, and what maximizes its benefit?
options: Per record on the broker; benefit is fixed
Per batch on the producer (compression.type); larger batches (linger.ms/batch.size) compress better
questions.explanation: The producer compresses whole batches before sending; brokers typically store them compressed and consumers decompress. Because compression works on batch payloads, better batching directly improves ratio and throughput.

questions.q: Which API creates topics, alters configurations, and lists consumer-group offsets programmatically?
questions.explanation: AdminClient is the management API for cluster resources: topics, configs, ACLs, consumer groups and their offsets. The Connect REST API manages connectors only.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application reads a topic with 8 partitions. The team deploys 10 instances (1 stream thread each) for throughput. What happens?
options: Only 8 instances get tasks — parallelism is capped by input partitions; 2 instances stay idle
questions.explanation: Streams creates one task per input partition; tasks are the unit of parallelism distributed across instances/threads sharing the application.id. Beyond 8 active workers there is nothing to assign.

questions.q: Which abstraction models 'the current price of each product, updated over time'?
options: KStream — every record is an independent event
KTable — a changelog where each key holds its latest value
questions.explanation: Latest-value-per-key semantics is exactly a KTable: new records for a key UPDATE the table. A KStream would treat every price change as a separate immutable event.

questions.q: A stream must be enriched with a small reference dataset (200 rows) on a key different from the stream's key, without repartitioning. Which construct fits?
options: GlobalKTable join — fully replicated to each instance and joinable on any key via a key mapper
questions.explanation: GlobalKTables are replicated in full to every instance, so joins don't require co-partitioning and can use an arbitrary join key. Perfect for small lookup data; regular KTable joins need same key + same partition count.

questions.q: What backs a Kafka Streams state store for fault tolerance?
options: A relational database configured by the user
A compacted changelog topic in Kafka; after failure, state is restored by replaying it (standby replicas shorten recovery)
A snapshot file uploaded to S3
Nothing — state is lost on failure
questions.explanation: Every state store writes its updates to a changelog topic. On task migration/failure the store is rebuilt from that topic; num.standby.replicas maintains warm copies to cut restore time.

questions.q: Count user clicks in fixed, non-overlapping 5-minute buckets. Which window type is this?
questions.explanation: Tumbling = fixed size, no overlap (each event in exactly one window). Hopping overlaps (size + smaller advance); session windows are activity-based with an inactivity gap; sliding relates records within a time difference.

questions.q: Group user activity into sessions that end after 30 minutes of inactivity. Which window applies?
options: Session windows with a 30-minute inactivity gap
questions.explanation: Session windows are dynamic: they extend while events keep arriving and close when the gap (30 min) elapses without activity — precisely the sessionization use case.

questions.q: What configuration turns on exactly-once processing in a Kafka Streams application?
questions.explanation: Streams wraps its consume-process-produce cycle (including changelog writes) in transactions when processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 is set — the one-line EOS switch. The other settings are pieces Streams manages internally.

questions.q: Before a stream-stream join, one stream is re-keyed with selectKey(). What does Streams do to make the join correct?
options: Writes the re-keyed stream through an internal repartition topic so equal keys land in the same task
Broadcasts all records to every task
questions.explanation: After a key change, records with the same new key may sit in different partitions. Streams inserts a repartition topic to shuffle them so join/aggregation tasks see all records for a key. (Joins also require equal partition counts — co-partitioning.)

questions.q: By default, which notion of time drives Kafka Streams windowing?
options: Wall-clock time when the record is processed
Event time from the record's timestamp (customizable with a TimestampExtractor)
questions.explanation: Streams windows on record timestamps (event time), so out-of-order and late records still fall into their correct windows (within the grace period). Processing time would make results depend on when the app happens to run.

questions.q: A windowed aggregation emits multiple intermediate updates per window, but the sink should receive only one final result per window. What is the idiomatic solution?
options: Use suppress() to hold updates until the window closes (grace period elapsed), emitting only the final value
Set linger.ms high on the producer
questions.explanation: Streams refines window results continuously; suppress(untilWindowCloses) buffers them and forwards only the final result when the window plus grace period ends.

questions.q: How does a Kafka Streams application scale out at runtime?
options: Start additional instances with the SAME application.id — tasks and their state migrate automatically to the new members
questions.explanation: application.id doubles as the consumer group id: new instances join the group, the tasks rebalance across members, and state stores are rebuilt/migrated (changelogs, standbys). Same mechanism in reverse handles instance loss.

questions.q: What is a Kafka Streams application, operationally?
options: A server process installed on each Kafka broker
A standard Java application using a client library — deployed like any app (VMs, Docker, Kubernetes), with no processing cluster to install
questions.explanation: Streams is a library, not a cluster: the topology runs inside your own JVM processes, coordinated through Kafka itself. Deployment is ordinary application deployment — which is exactly why it fits Docker/Kubernetes pipelines.

questions.q: Data must be copied continuously from a PostgreSQL database into Kafka, and from Kafka into Elasticsearch, with minimal custom code. What is the recommended approach?
options: Write a custom producer and consumer application
Kafka Connect: a source connector for PostgreSQL and a sink connector for Elasticsearch, both configuration-driven
A cron job with the console producer
questions.explanation: This is Kafka Connect's exact purpose: existing connectors handle offsets, retries, scaling, and schemas via configuration. Custom clients re-implement all of that; Streams is for processing, not system integration.

questions.q: Where does a DISTRIBUTED Kafka Connect cluster store connector configurations, source offsets, and status?
options: In internal Kafka topics (config, offset, and status topics) shared by the worker group
questions.explanation: Distributed mode keeps its state in Kafka itself, which is what makes workers stateless and fault-tolerant — any worker can pick up work. Standalone mode is the one using local files.

questions.q: How is a connector created and managed in a distributed Connect cluster?
options: Via the Connect REST API — POST the connector config; use it also for status, pause/resume, restart, delete
By placing a file on each worker
questions.explanation: Distributed Connect is managed at runtime over its REST interface (e.g., POST /connectors, GET /connectors/<name>/status). Standalone workers take properties files at startup instead.

questions.q: What is the relationship between a connector and its tasks?
options: The connector is the logical job definition; it splits the work into tasks (bounded by tasks.max) that run distributed across workers — the actual unit of parallelism
One task always equals one topic
questions.explanation: Connector = coordination + config; tasks = the parallel data-copying units. Effective parallelism is also limited by the source's divisible units (tables, files) or the topic's partitions for sinks.

questions.q: A JDBC source connector must write Avro records with schemas managed centrally. What configuration achieves this?
options: The JDBC connector always writes Avro
Set value.converter=io.confluent.connect.avro.AvroConverter with value.converter.schema.registry.url — converters are independent of the connector
questions.explanation: Converters translate Connect's internal records to bytes and are configured separately from the connector logic — the same connector can emit Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema, or plain JSON depending on converter settings.

questions.q: Field 'ssn' must be masked and records routed to a per-region topic as they flow through a connector — no external processing step. What mechanism does Connect provide?
options: Single Message Transforms (SMTs) chained in the connector config (e.g., MaskField + a routing transform)
questions.explanation: SMTs apply lightweight per-record transformations inside the connector pipeline. For heavier logic (joins, aggregation) you'd use a stream processor between source and sink instead.

questions.q: A sink connector keeps dying on malformed records. It must skip them but keep them for later inspection. Which configuration applies?
options: errors.tolerance=all with errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (and context headers enabled) — failed records go to a DLQ topic
questions.explanation: Tolerance 'all' keeps the task alive past bad records, and the DLQ topic (a SINK-connector feature) captures each failure — with reasons in headers when enabled — instead of silently dropping it.

questions.q: Compared with a polling JDBC source, what advantage does log-based CDC (e.g., Debezium) provide?
options: It requires no connector at all
It reads the database's transaction log: captures every change including DELETEs, with low latency and minimal load on tables
It works without the database running
It converts data to Avro automatically
questions.explanation: Polling misses deletes, adds query load, and is bounded by the poll interval. Reading the transaction log streams every committed row change (with before/after images), which is why CDC is preferred for database replication into Kafka.

questions.q: One worker in a 3-worker distributed Connect cluster crashes. What happens to the tasks it was running?
options: They are lost until the worker returns
The remaining workers rebalance and take over the failed worker's connectors/tasks, resuming from offsets stored in Kafka
All connectors restart from the beginning of history
The cluster stops accepting REST calls
questions.explanation: Because config/offsets/status live in Kafka topics, workers are interchangeable: the group rebalances the failed worker's tasks and they resume from their last committed source offsets.

questions.q: Which statement about standalone Connect workers is TRUE?
options: They are recommended for production for simplicity
They run in a single process with offsets in a local file — no fault tolerance or horizontal scaling; fine for development and testing
They store offsets in Kafka automatically
questions.explanation: Standalone = one process, local offset storage, no failover — a dev/test convenience. Production runs distributed mode for fault tolerance, scaling, and REST management.

questions.q: Producer callback logic (success metrics, error handling) must be unit-tested without a broker. Which tool fits?
options: MockProducer — records sent messages for assertions and can simulate send failures (errorNext)
questions.explanation: MockProducer is the in-memory test double from kafka-clients: assert on history(), drive callbacks with simulated success/failure — no broker, no network, deterministic.

questions.q: Which combination tests a Kafka Streams topology synchronously without any running Kafka cluster?
questions.explanation: TopologyTestDriver executes the topology in-process: pipe records via TestInputTopic, assert results from TestOutputTopic, and query state stores directly — fast, deterministic unit tests for Streams logic.

questions.q: A tumbling-window aggregation with a grace period must be tested deterministically, including late-arrival behavior. How?
options: Thread.sleep() in the test until the window closes
Use TopologyTestDriver's explicit time control — pipe records with chosen timestamps and advance wall-clock time to trigger window closing
Run it in production and watch
questions.explanation: The test driver lets you SET record timestamps and advance time programmatically, so window boundaries, grace periods, and suppress() emissions are exercised deterministically — no sleeps, no flakiness.

questions.q: Which risks are NOT covered by mocks and the TopologyTestDriver, and therefore need an integration test with a real broker (e.g., Testcontainers)?
options: Security handshakes, real Schema Registry (de)serialization, transactions, and rebalance behavior
questions.explanation: Interaction-level concerns — auth/TLS, registry round-trips, transactional semantics, group coordination — only exist against real infrastructure. Pure logic belongs in fast unit tests; keep both layers.

questions.q: How should schema changes be prevented from breaking production consumers?
options: Check candidate schemas against the registry's compatibility mode in CI (compatibility REST endpoint or schema-registry:test-compatibility Maven goal) so incompatible changes fail the build
questions.explanation: Schema Registry can validate a proposed schema against the subject's BACKWARD/FORWARD/FULL rules before anything is deployed — wiring that check into CI catches breaking changes at build time.

questions.q: What design choice makes producer/consumer application code easy to unit test?
options: Injecting the Producer/Consumer interface into the logic class, so tests pass MockProducer/MockConsumer and production passes real clients
questions.explanation: Dependency injection against the client INTERFACES is what lets the same logic run with mocks in tests. Hard-coded client construction forces integration tests for everything.

questions.q: What exactly is consumer lag for a partition?
options: The time since the consumer last polled
Log end offset minus the consumer's current/committed offset — how many records behind the consumer is
The number of rebalances per hour
questions.explanation: Lag counts unconsumed records between the partition's newest offset and the consumer's position. Persistent lag growth is THE signal that consumption can't keep up with production.

questions.q: How do Kafka clients expose their metrics for collection?
options: Via JMX MBeans (per client/topic/node), typically scraped by agents like the Prometheus JMX exporter
questions.explanation: Producer, consumer, Streams, and Connect all register JMX metrics out of the box; monitoring stacks scrape them. Streams adds thread/task/store metrics with configurable recording levels.

questions.q: Which producer metric indicates messages that ultimately FAILED delivery after exhausting retries, and what should its value be?
options: record-error-rate; it should be ~0 and alerted on otherwise
questions.explanation: record-error-rate counts definitive send failures — data loss from the app's perspective, so alert on any sustained non-zero value. record-retry-rate is the early-warning cousin: transient trouble being retried.

questions.q: Lag grows steadily on exactly ONE of a topic's 12 partitions. What is the most likely cause to investigate first?
options: A hot key skewing traffic to that partition (or one stuck/slow consumer instance) — check key distribution and that assignee's health
questions.explanation: Uniform problems raise lag everywhere; single-partition lag points to skew (one key getting disproportionate traffic) or the specific consumer assigned to it. Key cardinality/hashing is the classic culprit.

questions.q: Duplicates appear downstream after consumer crashes, even though processing is correct. The metrics show frequent rebalances. What is the explanation?
options: At-least-once consumption: records processed but not yet committed before the crash/rebalance are redelivered — make handling idempotent or adopt transactions/EOS
questions.explanation: Between processing and offset commit there is always a redelivery window under at-least-once. Frequent rebalances widen it. Fixes: idempotent writes keyed on record identity, or exactly-once processing where applicable.

questions.q: How can telemetry (counting, tracing) be added to every produced and consumed record WITHOUT modifying business code?
options: Configure ProducerInterceptor / ConsumerInterceptor classes via interceptor.classes — hooks that observe records on send and on consume
questions.explanation: Interceptors are the client-side extension point for cross-cutting observability (Confluent's monitoring interceptors use it). They see records in the send/consume path with zero business-code changes.

questions.q: A trace ID must follow one business transaction from the HTTP request, through the producer, into a consumer three services away. Where does the trace context travel in Kafka?
options: In the message key (replacing the business key)
In message HEADERS (e.g., W3C/OpenTelemetry trace context), propagated producer → consumer
questions.explanation: Headers are the metadata channel: tracing libraries inject context on produce and extract it on consume, keeping the trace intact — without abusing the key (which would change partitioning!).

questions.q: A producer starts throwing TimeoutException and bufferpool-wait-time climbs. What is happening?
options: The producer generates data faster than the cluster accepts it (or brokers are slow/unreachable): batches back up in buffer.memory until send() blocks and times out
questions.explanation: buffer.memory fills when the I/O thread can't drain batches fast enough — from broker slowness, network issues, or plain overproduction. Check request-latency and cluster health; consumer lag is unrelated to producer buffering.

questions.q: Which end-to-end health check catches pipeline delay that per-component metrics can miss?
options: End-to-end latency: compare the record's event timestamp with the time it is finally processed by the last consumer
questions.explanation: Each hop can look healthy while queueing between hops accumulates delay. Measuring event-time-to-final-processing spans the entire pipeline — the metric that reflects what the business actually experiences.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Fundamentals
objectives: Explain Kafka's data model: topics, partitions, offsets, and ordering
Describe brokers, replication, leaders/followers, and the ISR
Explain message keys, key hashing, and partition assignment
Describe consumer groups and how partitions are distributed
Explain data retention and log compaction
Compare delivery semantics: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once
Describe Kafka security fundamentals (encryption, authentication, authorization)
sections.notes:
Topics, partitions, and offsets

  • A topic is a named stream of records, split into partitions. Each partition is an ordered, immutable, append-only log; each record in it gets a sequential offset.
  • Ordering is guaranteed only WITHIN a partition — never across partitions of a topic. If a use case needs total order for a set of records (e.g. all events of one customer), those records must share a partition (⇒ same key).
  • Consumers track their position as the next offset to read per partition; reading does NOT remove data (Kafka is a log, not a queue) — many independent consumer groups read the same data.
  • Partition count = the topic's maximum parallelism for a consumer group. Partitions can be added later (breaks key→partition mapping for existing keys!) but never removed.
  • Records are key/value pairs plus a timestamp and optional headers (metadata for tracing, routing, schema hints — not used for partitioning).

Brokers, replication, and the ISR

  • A cluster is a set of brokers; each partition lives on brokers as one leader replica plus replication.factor − 1 follower replicas. All produce/consume traffic goes through the leader (followers replicate by fetching).
  • The ISR (in-sync replicas) is the set of replicas fully caught up with the leader. On leader failure, a new leader is elected from the ISR — no data loss if the ISR had the data.
  • min.insync.replicas (broker/topic config): the minimum ISR size for writes with acks=all to succeed. The classic durable setup: RF=3, min.insync.replicas=2, acks=all — tolerates one broker down while never acknowledging a write held by fewer than 2 replicas; producers get NotEnoughReplicasException if the ISR shrinks below 2.
  • Unclean leader election (electing a non-ISR replica) trades data loss for availability; it is disabled by default.

Message keys and partition assignment

  • Producer partitioning: key present → partition = murmur2(key) % numPartitions — the same key ALWAYS lands on the same partition (ordering per key). Key = null → sticky partitioning: the producer fills a batch to one random partition, then switches (efficient round-robin-ish distribution).
  • A producer can also set the partition explicitly or plug a custom Partitioner.
  • Design consequence: choose keys with enough cardinality to spread load; a low-cardinality key (e.g. country) creates hot partitions. Changing partition count remaps keys — plan partitions up front for keyed topics.

Consumer groups

  • Consumers with the same group.id form a consumer group: each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. More consumers than partitions ⇒ the extras sit idle.
  • Different groups are independent — each receives every record (pub-sub across groups, queue-like within a group).
  • Group membership is coordinated by a broker acting as group coordinator; consumers heartbeat, and joins/leaves/failures trigger a rebalance (partition reassignment). Committed offsets are stored in the internal __consumer_offsets topic.

Retention and log compaction

Cleanup policyBehaviorUse case
delete (default)Segments older than retention.ms (default 7 days) or beyond retention.bytes are deleted — regardless of consumptionEvent streams, telemetry
compactKeeps at least the latest record per key; older values for the same key are eventually removed; a null value ("tombstone") deletes the keyChangelogs, table-like state (latest value per entity)
compact,deleteBoth: compaction plus time/size-based expiryBounded changelogs

Retention is per topic; consumers must keep up or re-read from what still exists (auto.offset.reset decides where a group starts with no valid offset: earliest or latest).

Delivery semantics

SemanticsHow it happensCost
At-most-onceCommit/ack BEFORE processing (or producer with acks=0) — failures lose data, never duplicateData loss possible
At-least-onceProcess THEN commit; producer retries — failures re-deliverDuplicates possible ⇒ consumers should be idempotent
Exactly-onceIdempotent producer + transactions (Kafka-to-Kafka), consumer isolation.level=read_committedSome latency/throughput overhead; EOS applies to Kafka↔Kafka pipelines (external sinks still need idempotency)

Kafka security basics

  • Encryption in transit: TLS between clients and brokers (and inter-broker); listeners defined per protocol (PLAINTEXT, SSL, SASL_SSL…).
  • Authentication: SASL mechanisms — PLAIN (username/password, over TLS!), SCRAM-SHA-256/512, GSSAPI (Kerberos), OAUTHBEARER — or mutual TLS (client certificates).
  • Authorization: ACLs per principal on resources (topic, group, cluster) and operations (Read, Write, Create…). A typical client needs Read on the topic + Read on its consumer group, or Write on the topic to produce.
  • Clients configure this with security.protocol, sasl.mechanism, sasl.jaas.config, plus truststore/keystore settings.

KRaft and the control plane

Kafka's metadata (topics, partitions, leaders, configs) was historically coordinated by ZooKeeper; modern Kafka uses KRaft — brokers/controllers run the Raft consensus protocol internally, removing ZooKeeper entirely (default since Kafka 4.0). For a developer the client APIs are unchanged; know that cluster metadata and controller election are internal (a controller quorum) and that administration happens via the Admin API/CLI tools.

Exam tip: the most-tested fundamentals: ordering only within a partition; same key → same partition; consumers > partitions ⇒ idle consumers; RF=3 + min.insync.replicas=2 + acks=all tolerates exactly one broker loss; compaction keeps the latest value per key (tombstones delete). Memorize these cold.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Application Development
objectives: Configure and tune producers (acks, batching, retries, idempotence)
Implement exactly-once semantics with idempotent producers and transactions
Configure and tune consumers (poll loop, offset commits, rebalancing)
Use serializers/deserializers and Schema Registry with Avro/JSON/Protobuf
Handle retriable and non-retriable errors in clients
Use the Admin API and command-line tools
Apply compression and batching for throughput
sections.notes:
The producer: send path and key configs

send() is asynchronous: records go through the (key/value) serializer → partitioner → an in-memory batch per partition (buffer.memory), and a background I/O thread ships batches. A callback (or the returned Future) reports success (RecordMetadata) or failure.

ConfigMeaning
acks0 = fire-and-forget; 1 = leader has written it; all = all in-sync replicas have it (durable; pair with min.insync.replicas)
retries / delivery.timeout.msAutomatic retry of transient failures; delivery.timeout.ms bounds the whole attempt
enable.idempotenceBroker de-duplicates producer retries (sequence numbers) — no duplicates from retries, per-partition ordering preserved; default true in modern clients
max.in.flight.requests.per.connectionConcurrent unacked requests; with idempotence ≤5 ordering is still guaranteed
linger.ms / batch.sizeWait up to linger.ms to fill batches up to batch.size — the main throughput/latency trade-off knobs
compression.typenone / gzip / snappy / lz4 / zstd — compresses whole batches

Idempotence and transactions (EOS)

  • Idempotent producer = no duplicates from retries on one producer session. It does NOT dedupe application-level re-sends.
  • Transactions: set a stable transactional.id, then initTransactions() → beginTransaction() → send(...) + sendOffsetsToTransaction(...) → commitTransaction(). Enables atomic consume-transform-produce: output records and consumed-offset commits succeed or abort together.
  • Downstream consumers must set isolation.level=read_committed to skip aborted records (default is read_uncommitted).
  • Exactly-once applies Kafka-to-Kafka; writing to an external DB still needs idempotent writes or its own transaction coordination.

The consumer: poll loop, commits, rebalancing

while (true) {
  ConsumerRecords<K,V> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(250));
  for (ConsumerRecord<K,V> rec : records) process(rec);
  consumer.commitSync();          // manual at-least-once
}
  • Offset commits: enable.auto.commit=true commits every auto.commit.interval.ms during poll (risk: commits records not yet processed → possible loss on crash). Manual commitSync() (blocking, retried) or commitAsync() (fast, no retry) AFTER processing gives at-least-once.
  • Liveness: heartbeats (background thread) within session.timeout.ms keep membership; max.poll.interval.ms caps time between polls — exceed it (slow processing) and the group evicts the consumer, causing a rebalance. Tune it, or lower max.poll.records.
  • Rebalancing: assignment strategies include range, round-robin, sticky, and cooperative-sticky (incremental — only moved partitions stop, no global stop-the-world). A ConsumerRebalanceListener lets you commit/cleanup on revocation.
  • auto.offset.reset (earliest/latest/none) applies only when the group has NO valid committed offset.
  • Consumers are not thread-safe — one consumer per thread; scale by adding consumers (up to partition count).

Serialization and Schema Registry

  • Producers serialize keys/values to bytes; consumers must deserialize with a compatible scheme. Common formats: Avro, JSON Schema, Protobuf (all supported by Confluent SerDes) — plus primitives (String, Long…).
  • Schema Registry stores versioned schemas per subject (default: <topic>-key / <topic>-value); the wire format is a magic byte + schema ID + payload — consumers fetch schemas by ID and cache them.
  • Compatibility modes govern schema evolution:
ModeNew schema can be used to read…Safe changesUpgrade order
BACKWARD (default)data written with the previous schemadelete fields, add optional fields (with defaults)Consumers first
FORWARDold schema reads data written with the new oneadd fields, delete optional fieldsProducers first
FULLboth directionsonly add/remove optional fields with defaultsAny order

*_TRANSITIVE variants check against ALL previous versions, not just the latest.

Error handling: retriable vs non-retriable

Retriable (client retries automatically)Non-retriable (fix required)
LeaderNotAvailable / NotLeaderForPartition, NetworkException, NotEnoughReplicas, timeouts — transient cluster eventsSerializationException, RecordTooLargeException (> max.request.size / message.max.bytes), AuthorizationException, InvalidTopicException

Producer: rely on retries + idempotence for the left column; catch and handle (log/DLQ/alert) the right. Consumer: a poison record that fails deserialization will fail on EVERY poll — catch it, skip past it (seek beyond, or use an error-handling deserializer) and route it to a dead-letter topic.

Admin API and CLI tools

  • AdminClient: programmatic create/describe/delete topics, alter configs, list consumer groups and their offsets/lag, manage ACLs.
  • CLI equivalents: kafka-topics.sh (--create --partitions --replication-factor / --describe), kafka-console-producer.sh / kafka-console-consumer.sh (--from-beginning, --group), kafka-consumer-groups.sh (--describe shows per-partition LAG; --reset-offsets), kafka-configs.sh.

Compression and batching

  • Compression is set on the producer (whole-batch compression); brokers usually store batches as-is; consumers decompress. Bigger batches (higher linger.ms/batch.size) compress better — compounding throughput gains at a small latency cost.
  • zstd/lz4 are the modern favorites (good ratio/CPU balance); gzip best ratio, most CPU.

Exam tip: favorite questions — what acks=all requires (paired min.insync.replicas); idempotence stopping retry duplicates but transactions being needed for consume-transform-produce EOS; auto-commit's data-loss window vs manual commit-after-processing; max.poll.interval.ms evictions on slow processing; BACKWARD compatibility = consumers upgrade first; and the retriable/non-retriable split.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Streams
objectives: Describe the Streams processing topology, tasks, and threading model
Choose between KStream, KTable, and GlobalKTable
Use stateful operations: aggregations, state stores, and internal topics
Apply windowing (tumbling, hopping, sliding, session) and time semantics
Join streams and tables correctly
Configure processing guarantees (at-least-once, exactly-once)
sections.notes:
Topology, tasks, and threads

  • Kafka Streams is a client library (plain Java app — no cluster to install; deploy like any app, e.g. in Docker/Kubernetes). A topology is the graph of sources → processors → sinks built with the DSL (StreamsBuilder) or Processor API.
  • The topology is split into tasks — one per input partition — the unit of parallelism. Tasks are distributed over num.stream.threads and over all app instances sharing the same application.id (which is also the consumer group id). Max parallelism = input partition count; extra instances/threads idle.
  • Scaling out = start another instance with the same application.id: tasks (and their state) migrate automatically.

KStream, KTable, GlobalKTable

KStreamKTableGlobalKTable
SemanticsEvent stream — every record is an independent fact ("orders placed")Changelog — latest value per key ("current price per product"); updates overwriteLike KTable but FULLY replicated to every instance
Reads topicAll partitions split across tasksAll partitions split across tasksEvery instance reads ALL partitions
Join keyingStream-stream and stream-table joins require co-partitioning (same key, same partition count)Join on any key (via mapper) — no co-partitioning needed
Typical useTransformations, enrichment eventsAggregation results, reference data that updatesSmall, broadly needed lookup data

Stateful processing: state stores and internal topics

  • Stateful operations (count, aggregate, reduce, joins, windowing) keep local state stores (RocksDB on disk by default, in-memory optional).
  • Every state store is backed by a compacted changelog topic in Kafka — the source of truth. On instance failure/migration, state is restored by replaying the changelog; num.standby.replicas keeps warm copies to shorten failover.
  • Re-keying operations (groupBy, selectKey before an aggregation/join) create internal repartition topics so records with the same new key meet in the same task.
  • State is queryable in-process (interactive queries) for serving lookups without an external DB.

Windowing and time

WindowShapeExample
TumblingFixed size, non-overlappingCount per 5 minutes
HoppingFixed size, overlapping (size + advance)5-min window advancing every 1 min
SlidingBound to record pairs within a time differenceJoins/aggregations over "within 30 s of each other"
SessionActivity-based, closed by an inactivity gapUser sessions with 10-min idle gap
  • Streams operates on event time by default (record timestamps; custom TimestampExtractor possible), not wall-clock arrival time.
  • Late/out-of-order records within the window's grace period still update it; results are continuously refined (use suppress() to emit only the final result when a window closes).

Joins

  • Stream–stream joins are always windowed (records must occur within the window) and support inner/left/outer.
  • Stream–table joins enrich events with the table's current value for the key (inner/left).
  • Table–table joins produce an updating joined table.
  • Except with GlobalKTables, join inputs must be co-partitioned: same key space and the SAME number of partitions — otherwise Streams throws or requires an explicit repartition.

Processing guarantees

  • processing.guarantee=at_least_once (default) — duplicates possible after failures.
  • processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 — wraps the consume-process-produce cycle (including state-store changelogs) in Kafka transactions; requires brokers ≥ 2.5, adds modest overhead. This is the one-line EOS switch for Streams apps.

Exam tip: classic questions — tasks = input partitions (adding instances beyond that does nothing); KTable = latest value per key (vs KStream = every event); GlobalKTable = fully replicated, no co-partitioning; state restored from changelog topics; exactly_once_v2 as the EOS setting; tumbling vs hopping vs session recognition from a scenario.

sections.title: Kafka Connect
objectives: Explain Kafka Connect's purpose: source and sink integration without custom code
Compare standalone and distributed worker deployments
Configure connectors and scale them with tasks
Choose converters and integrate Schema Registry
Apply Single Message Transforms
Configure error tolerance and dead letter queues
Describe CDC (change data capture) with source connectors
sections.notes:
What Connect is and when to use it

  • Kafka Connect is the framework for configuration-driven integration between Kafka and external systems: source connectors pull data INTO Kafka; sink connectors push data OUT of Kafka. No custom producer/consumer code — you deploy a connector plugin and give it a JSON/properties config.
  • Connect handles the hard parts for you: offset tracking (where the source left off / what the sink has written), scaling, fault tolerance, retries, schemas, and transformation hooks. Prefer it over hand-written glue code whenever a connector exists.

Workers: standalone vs distributed

StandaloneDistributed
ProcessesOne worker processCluster of workers sharing a group.id
Config/offsets storedLocal filesInternal Kafka topics (config, offsets, status)
Fault tolerance / scalingNone — the process IS the systemWork is rebalanced across workers; add workers to scale
ManagementProperties file at startupREST API (create/pause/resume/delete connectors, view status)
UseDev, testing, one-off local jobsAnything production

Connectors, tasks, and scaling

  • A connector instance is the logical job (e.g. "this database → these topics"); it divides work into tasks — the actual units that copy data, spread across workers.
  • tasks.max caps parallelism; the effective task count also depends on the source's dividable units (e.g. number of tables, or a JDBC source may cap at one task) and, for sinks, the topic's partitions.
  • Failed tasks can be restarted via the REST API (/connectors/<name>/status, /restart).

Converters and Schema Registry

  • Converters translate between Connect's internal data representation and the bytes in Kafka: key.converter / value.converter set per worker or per connector. Common: AvroConverter, ProtobufConverter, JsonSchemaConverter (all with ...converter.schema.registry.url), plain JsonConverter (optionally with embedded schemas), StringConverter.
  • Converters are chosen INDEPENDENTLY of the connector — the same JDBC source can write Avro or JSON depending on converter config. Mismatched converters between producer side and consumer side are the classic "deserialization garbage" root cause.

Single Message Transforms (SMTs)

  • Lightweight, per-record transformations applied in the connector pipeline (source: before writing to Kafka; sink: before writing to the target): rename/mask/insert fields, route records to different topics, cast types, extract keys, filter records.
  • Configured as a chain: transforms=mask,route with per-transform classes/settings. For anything heavier (joins, aggregations, lookups) use a stream processor (Kafka Streams) between source and sink instead of stretching SMTs.

Error handling and dead letter queues

  • errors.tolerance=none (default — task fails on a bad record) vs all (skip and continue).
  • Sink connectors can route failed records to a dead letter queue topic: errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (+ context.headers.enable to attach failure reasons as headers). DLQs apply to sinks, not sources.
  • errors.retry.timeout enables retries of transient failures before tolerance/DLQ handling kicks in.

Change Data Capture (CDC)

  • CDC source connectors (e.g. Debezium) read a database's transaction log (not query polling) and publish every row-level INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE as an event — low latency, no load on tables, captures deletes.
  • Contrast with JDBC source polling (periodic SELECTs with an incrementing/timestamp column): simpler, but misses deletes, adds DB load, and has poll-interval latency.
  • CDC events conventionally carry before/after images; deletes are followed by tombstones for compacted topics — pairing naturally with KTable-style consumption.

Exam tip: distributed workers store config/offsets/status in internal Kafka topics and are managed over REST — standalone uses local files; that contrast is heavily tested. So are: tasks as the unit of parallelism (tasks.max), converters being independent of connectors, DLQs being a SINK feature, and log-based CDC vs polling trade-offs.

sections.title: Application Testing
objectives: Unit test producer and consumer logic with mocks
Test Kafka Streams topologies with TopologyTestDriver
Design integration tests against real (containerized/embedded) brokers
Test schema compatibility before deployment
sections.notes:
Unit testing producers and consumers

  • The Kafka clients library ships test doubles: MockProducer and MockConsumer — no broker needed.
  • MockProducer records everything your code sends (history()) and can simulate success or failure of sends (errorNext(exception)) — assert what was produced, to which topic/partition, with which key, and that callbacks/error paths behave.
  • MockConsumer lets tests seed assigned partitions, records, and end offsets, then verifies the poll-loop logic: processing, commit behavior, rebalance listener handling.
  • Structure application code so the Kafka client is injected — logic classes take a Producer<K,V>/Consumer<K,V> interface, tests pass the mock, production passes the real client.

Testing Kafka Streams topologies

TopologyTestDriver driver = new TopologyTestDriver(builder.build(), props);
TestInputTopic<String, Order> in =
    driver.createInputTopic("orders", stringSerde.serializer(), orderSerde.serializer());
TestOutputTopic<String, Long> out =
    driver.createOutputTopic("order-counts", stringSerde.deserializer(), longSerde.deserializer());

in.pipeInput("cust-1", order1);
in.pipeInput("cust-1", order2);
assertEquals(2L, out.readKeyValue().value);   // synchronous, deterministic
  • TopologyTestDriver executes the topology synchronously without any broker: pipe records into TestInputTopics, read results from TestOutputTopics, and query state stores directly (driver.getKeyValueStore(...)).
  • It controls time explicitly — advance wall-clock/event time to test windowing, grace periods, and suppression deterministically (no sleeps, no flakiness).

Integration testing with real brokers

  • Unit tests can't cover: real serialization against a Schema Registry, security handshakes, rebalancing behavior, transactions, connector configs. For those, run a REAL Kafka in the test: Testcontainers (Kafka container per test class — today's standard), embedded Kafka utilities, or a shared test cluster.
  • Keep them separate from unit tests (slower, resource-heavy); make topics unique per run; assert with consumers polling until a timeout rather than fixed sleeps.
  • Test failure modes deliberately: kill the container to observe retry/idempotence behavior; produce poison records to verify DLQ paths.

Testing schema evolution

  • Before deploying a producer with a changed schema, verify compatibility against the registry: the Schema Registry compatibility check REST endpoint (or Maven plugin schema-registry:test-compatibility) validates a candidate schema against the subject's configured mode (BACKWARD/FORWARD/FULL) — wire this into CI so incompatible changes fail the build, not production consumers.

Exam tip: map tool to layer — MockProducer/MockConsumer for client logic, TopologyTestDriver (with TestInputTopic/TestOutputTopic) for Streams topologies without a broker, Testcontainers/embedded Kafka for integration concerns, and registry compatibility checks in CI for schema changes. TopologyTestDriver's deterministic time control for window testing is a favorite question.

sections.title: Application Observability
objectives: Expose and collect client metrics (JMX) for producers, consumers, and Streams
Monitor consumer lag and alert on it
Track producer health: errors, retries, latency, throughput
Use logging, tracing, and interceptors for end-to-end visibility
Troubleshoot common client-side problems from metrics
sections.notes:
Client metrics via JMX

  • All Kafka clients (producer, consumer, Streams, Connect) expose metrics through JMX MBeans; scrape them with your monitoring stack (Prometheus JMX exporter, etc.). Metrics exist per client, per topic, and per node.
  • Kafka Streams adds thread/task/processor/state-store metrics (metrics.recording.level controls granularity); Connect exposes connector/task status and throughput metrics.
  • Watch BOTH sides: client metrics tell you how YOUR app behaves; broker-side metrics (under-replicated partitions, request handler idle %) tell you about the cluster — a developer is expected to read the client side fluently.

Consumer lag — the number-one signal

  • Lag = log end offset − committed/current offset, per partition: how far behind the consumer is. Growing lag means the app can't keep up (or stopped).
  • Sources: consumer metric records-lag-max, kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group app (shows CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET, LAG per partition), and external lag exporters (e.g. Burrow) for fleet-wide monitoring.
  • Alert on sustained growth, not momentary spikes. Remedies: faster processing (batch/parallelize the work), more consumers (up to partition count), more partitions (planned!), or tuning max.poll.records/processing pipeline.
  • Also watch rebalance metrics (rebalance rate, time-between-poll) — frequent rebalances often masquerade as lag problems.

Producer health metrics

MetricWhat it tells you
record-error-rateSends that ultimately FAILED (post-retries) — should be ~0; alert otherwise
record-retry-rateTransient trouble being retried — early warning of cluster/network issues
request-latency-avg/maxBroker round-trip time — rising latency precedes buffer exhaustion
batch-size-avg, records-per-request-avg, compression-rate-avgBatching efficiency — small batches = tune linger.ms/batch.size
buffer-available-bytes / bufferpool-wait-timebuffer.memory pressure — send() will start blocking (max.block.ms) when exhausted

Logging, tracing, and interceptors

  • Client logs (log4j/slf4j) surface warnings you should not ignore: rebalances, disconnections, expired transactions, slow commits.
  • Message headers carry correlation/trace IDs across the pipeline; standard practice is W3C/OpenTelemetry trace context propagated producer → consumer so one business transaction is traceable end to end.
  • Interceptors (ProducerInterceptor / ConsumerInterceptor) hook onSend/onConsume for cross-cutting telemetry (count, tag, trace) without touching business code — this is how Confluent Monitoring interceptors work.
  • End-to-end latency = event timestamp vs processing time at the consumer — measure it; per-hop metrics alone hide pipeline delays.

Troubleshooting common scenarios

SymptomLikely cause → check
Lag grows on ALL partitionsProcessing too slow overall → processing time per record, thread pool, downstream calls
Lag grows on ONE partitionHot key/skewed partitioning, or one stuck consumer → key distribution, that consumer's logs
Consumer keeps leaving the grouppoll() gaps exceed max.poll.interval.ms → reduce max.poll.records or speed up processing
Duplicates downstreamAt-least-once + rebalances/crashes between processing and commit → idempotent handling or EOS/transactions
Producer TimeoutException, buffer fullCluster slow/unreachable or produce rate > capacity → request latency, cluster health, buffer.memory
Consumer gets garbage / DeserializationExceptionSerializer/converter mismatch or schema change → producer serializer vs consumer deserializer, registry compatibility

Exam tip: consumer LAG is the metric the exam cares most about — know its definition, where to read it (metrics + kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe), and the remediation options. Producer-side, record-error-rate vs record-retry-rate (failed vs retried) is the classic pair. And every "consumer evicted from group" scenario resolves to max.poll.interval.ms.
